Introduction

What is a Self-Balancing Robot?

A self-balancing robot is a dynamically unstable robotic system capable of maintaining an upright position through continuous sensing and control.

The fundamental principle is the inverted pendulum on a cart — a classic benchmark in robotics and control theory. This project bridges theoretical dynamics with real-world robotic control implementation.

Motivation

Why This Project Matters

01 / 04
Real-World Unstable Control
Self-balancing robots directly apply unstable system control — a core topic in modern robotics and control engineering.
02 / 04
Academic Benchmark
The inverted pendulum is the standard benchmark for validating control algorithms, making this project academically significant.
03 / 04
Control Theory in Practice
Bridges theoretical control engineering with hands-on implementation — stabilizing an inherently unstable physical system.
04 / 04
Industry-Scale Versatility
Foundation of Segways, humanoid robots, delivery bots, and SpaceX rocket landing systems — all use this control principle.
Objectives

Project Objectives

  • Design and develop a two-wheeled self-balancing robot platform.
  • Measure tilt angle using an MPU6050 IMU sensor with 6-axis motion tracking.
  • Implement a PID control algorithm for real-time balance correction.
  • Control DC motors through a motor driver based on PID output signals.
Essential Components

Hardware Components Used

The self-balancing robot relies on carefully selected hardware components that work together to sense orientation, process control algorithms, and drive the motors with precision.

Arduino Uno R3
Microcontroller brain that executes the control algorithm, reads sensor data, and commands motor drivers at high frequency.
MPU6050 IMU Sensor
6-axis accelerometer and gyroscope that measures tilt angle and angular velocity for real-time orientation feedback.
L298N Motor Driver
H-bridge driver that amplifies control signals from the Arduino to drive DC motors in both forward and reverse directions.
Batteries & DC Motors
Power source and actuators that provide electrical energy and convert control signals into mechanical motion for balancing.
Ultrasonic Sensor & Servo Motor
Distance measurement sensor and precision actuator for obstacle detection and additional control capabilities.
Problem Statement

The Control Challenge

Core Problem Maintaining balance in two-wheeled robotic systems is inherently challenging. Conventional robots rely on multiple support points; a two-wheeled robot must continuously sense its tilt and apply corrective motor actions in real time.

This project addresses designing and implementing a robot that autonomously maintains an upright position using sensor feedback and a closed-loop control algorithm — entirely without external human intervention during operation.

Core Theory

The Inverted Pendulum Concept

An inverted pendulum has its center of mass above the pivot point, making it naturally unstable. Unlike a standard pendulum it will not return to equilibrium without active external control.

INSTABILITY 01
Gravitational Toppling Torque
Gravity continuously applies a torque that amplifies any deviation from upright — exponentially without correction.
INSTABILITY 02
Center of Mass Location
CoM above pivot means any angular perturbation grows unstably — constant active feedback is the only solution.
INSTABILITY 03
Mathematical Instability
System eigenvalues lie in the right-half complex plane — only active feedback can relocate poles to stable positions.
Stabilization Strategy

How We Stabilize the System

An active feedback control loop continuously computes corrective forces to keep the center of mass above the wheels. Three steps form this loop:

STEP 01
Tilt Angle Measurement
MPU6050 continuously measures orientation. This tilt angle is the primary feedback parameter for balance control.
STEP 02
Closed-Loop Feedback
PID controller processes the error between desired angle (0°) and measured angle, computing the corrective signal.
STEP 03
Motor Actuation
Motor driver translates PWM signals from the controller into physical torque to correct the tilt.
Sensor System

Tilt Measurement — MPU6050

The MPU6050 is a 6-axis Motion Tracking device combining a 3-axis accelerometer and a 3-axis gyroscope, providing complete 3D orientation data.

3-Axis Accelerometer (Long-Term Stabilizer)

Uses MEMS technology — a microscopic mass on a spring. When the robot tilts, the mass shifts due to gravity, changing capacitance between internal silicon structures. The Arduino calculates tilt angle from gravity components across all three axes.

G-ForceRaw_g = Raw_Value / Scale_Factor
Pitchθ_pitch = atan(Ax / √(Ay² + Az²)) × (180/π)
Rollϕ_roll = atan(Ay / √(Ax² + Az²)) × (180/π)
Gyroscope

Gyroscope Formulas

The gyroscope measures angular velocity (ω) — the rate of rotation. Fast, smooth response but accumulates drift error over time.

Raw to °/sω (°/s) = Raw_Gyro / Scale_Factor
IntegrationAngle(t) = Angle(t-1) + ω × Δt
Known Limitation — Drift Integrating tiny measurement errors each millisecond causes the angle to slowly drift from true value — requiring sensor fusion correction.
Sensor Fusion

The Complementary Filter

Accelerometer is noisy; gyroscope drifts. The complementary filter fuses both sensors — taking the best of each for a clean, accurate angle estimate.

Gold StandardAngle = α·(Angle + ω·Δt) + (1−α)·Accel_Angle
Filter Coefficient α = 0.98 High-pass (0.98 × Gyro): Captures fast, smooth motion.

Low-pass (0.02 × Accel): Slowly corrects gyro drift back to true gravity vector.
Advanced Processing

DMP & Quaternions

The onboard Digital Motion Processor computes quaternions — 4D complex numbers (qw, qx, qy, qz) representing orientation without gimbal lock.

Why Quaternions?Standard Euler angles suffer from Gimbal Lock — two axes aligning, losing a rotational degree of freedom. Quaternions represent all orientations without singularity.
Gravity Vectorgx = 2(qx·qz − qw·qy)
gy = 2(qw·qx + qy·qz)
gz = qw² − qx² − qy² + qz²
Practical AdvantageThe DMP offloads heavy computation from the Arduino CPU, delivering accurate quaternion-based orientation at high update rates — critical for real-time balance control.
Control Architecture

Closed-Loop Feedback System

  • Robot operates using closed-loop feedback control at all times.
  • Output (tilt angle) continuously measured and fed back into the system.
  • Controller compares measured angle with the reference (0° upright).
  • Corrective action applied proportionally based on the computed error.
Feedback DefinitionUsing the system's own output to influence its control input — enabling autonomous self-correction.
Error Signal

Reference, Output & Error

  • Desired reference angle fixed at 0° — perfect upright.
  • Measured output is the estimated tilt angle from sensor fusion.
  • Error signal represents deviation from the reference angle.
Error Equatione(t) = θ_ref − θ_measured = 0° − θ_measured
System Architecture

Controller & Plant

CONTROLLER
PID Algorithm
Processes the error signal using three mathematical terms. Its computed output directly drives the plant in real time.
PLANT
Physical Robotic System
Represents motors, wheels, chassis, and body. Receives controller output and responds with physical motion measured back as new sensor data.
PID Control

PID Controller

Classical feedback control algorithm combining three actions. The gold standard for inverted pendulum stabilisation.

Control Lawu(t) = Kp·e(t) + Ki·∫e(t)dt + Kd·(de/dt)
P
Proportional
Output proportional to current error. Primary restoring force — larger tilt means stronger correction.
P TermP_out = Kp × e(t)
LimitationCauses oscillation if used alone.
I
Integral
Accumulates past error. Eliminates steady-state error and corrects bias from motor mismatch or slope.
I TermI_out = Ki × ∫e(t)dt
Practical NoteKept small; sometimes disabled.
D
Derivative
Responds to rate of change of error. Detects falling speed and adds damping to prevent overshoot.
D TermD_out = Kd × ω_gyro
Key InsightOpposes motion, not position.
Actuation

Motor Driver & Actuation

STAGE 01
Controller → PWM Signal
PID output converts to a Pulse Width Modulation signal encoding motor speed and direction.
STAGE 02
Motor Driver Amplification
The motor driver (L298N) amplifies the low-current microcontroller signal to provide sufficient current for DC motor operation.
STAGE 03
Torque → Tilt Correction
Motors generate physical torque to drive wheels in the corrective direction, counteracting the robot's lean.
Important DistinctionThe controller computes required corrective torque mathematically. The motor driver and motors physically execute that correction — completing one control cycle typically every 5–20ms.
Complete System

The Complete Control Loop

  • Sensors (accelerometer + gyroscope) measure robot motion state.
  • Angle estimation via complementary filter or DMP quaternions.
  • Error computed: Reference (0°) minus measured tilt angle.
  • PID controller computes corrective PWM signal from error.
  • Motors apply torque to physically correct the tilt in real time.
  • Loop repeats continuously — every 5–20 milliseconds.
Construction Guide

First Steps to Build the Self-Balancing Robot

Hardware Components

  • Arduino Uno or compatible microcontroller board
  • MPU6050 6-axis gyroscope and accelerometer sensor
  • Two DC motors with wheels (ideally geared motors)
  • Motor driver module (L298N or similar H-bridge)
  • Chassis frame or 3D-printed robot body
  • Battery pack (7.4V LiPo or 9V rechargeable recommended)
  • Jumper wires and breadboard for initial prototyping

Assembly Steps

  • Step 1: Mount motors vertically on the chassis ensuring parallel alignment
  • Step 2: Attach wheels to motors — ensure equal diameter and secure fit
  • Step 3: Install MPU6050 sensor at robot's center of mass
  • Step 4: Connect motor driver to Arduino and motors following pinout diagram
  • Step 5: Wire MPU6050 to Arduino via I2C (SDA, SCL pins)
  • Step 6: Connect battery pack to power distribution ensuring stable voltage
  • Step 7: Upload initial calibration code to test sensor readings
  • Step 8: Implement PID control loop and iteratively tune gains
Critical Values

Key Parameters that Impact Performance

PARAMETER 01
Setpoint (Target Angle)
The desired vertical angle, typically 0° for upright balance. Small adjustments (±2°) compensate for mechanical imbalances or weight distribution. Incorrect setpoint causes constant drift in one direction.
PARAMETER 02
Gyro Offset Values
Sensor calibration offsets correct MPU6050 drift and bias. Measured during initialization while robot is stationary. Typical values: Accel X/Y: ±200, Gyro Z: ±50. Poor calibration causes cumulative angle error.
PARAMETER 03
PID Constants (Kp, Ki, Kd)
Kp (Proportional): Immediate response to tilt (typical: 40-60)
Ki (Integral): Eliminates steady-state error (typical: 0-5)
Kd (Derivative): Damping to prevent oscillation (typical: 0.5-2.0)
Tuning order: Kp → Kd → Ki for optimal stability.
PARAMETER 04
Center of Mass Alignment
Robot's center of gravity must align vertically with wheel axle. Battery placement critically affects balance — mount low and centered. Misalignment by >5mm requires constant motor correction, draining battery and reducing stability margins.

Tuning Guidelines

  • Start with Kp only — increase until stable oscillation occurs
  • Reduce Kp by 20-30% and add Kd to dampen oscillations
  • Add small Ki only if steady-state drift persists
  • Re-calibrate gyro offsets if angle drifts over time

Common Issues

  • Oscillation: Kp too high or Kd too low
  • Slow response: Kp too low
  • Drift: Incorrect setpoint or gyro offset error
  • Instability: Center of mass misalignment
Real-World Problems

Key Challenges Faced While Building the Self-Balancing Robot

CHALLENGE 01
Motor Torque Was Not Sufficient
Standard hobby DC motors often lack adequate torque for rapid corrections. Heavy robot weight and high center of gravity demand stronger motors with proper gear ratios (typically 1:50 to 1:100). Solution: Use geared motors with at least 5-10 kg-cm torque or upgrade to stepper/brushless motors.
CHALLENGE 02
Battery Voltage Drop
Motors draw significant current during balance corrections causing voltage sag. Voltage drop below 6V reduces motor torque and responsiveness, leading to unstable behavior. Solution: Use high-discharge LiPo batteries (2S 7.4V, 2000mAh+) with low internal resistance and add voltage monitoring.
CHALLENGE 03
PID Parameter Tuning
Finding optimal Kp, Ki, Kd values is iterative and time-consuming. Each robot requires unique tuning due to mass, motor specs, and mechanical differences. Aggressive tuning causes oscillation; conservative tuning results in slow, unstable response. Solution: Use Ziegler-Nichols method or systematic tuning (start Kp=60, Kd=6.0, Ki=0).
CHALLENGE 04
MPU6050 Sensor Noise
Raw accelerometer and gyroscope readings contain significant noise and vibration interference. Motor vibrations amplify sensor noise creating false tilt readings. Solution: Enable MPU6050 Digital Motion Processor (DMP) for quaternion-based filtering, or implement complementary filter. Mount sensor on vibration-dampening foam.
CHALLENGE 05
Weight Distribution and Center of Gravity
Improper weight distribution causes continuous drift and requires constant motor correction. Battery placement critically affects CoG — top-heavy designs increase instability. Even 5mm CoG offset from wheel axle demands significant corrective torque. Solution: Mount battery low and centered, position heavy components near wheel axle, test balance manually before powering.
Virtual Testing

MATLAB PID Simulation — Tilt Angle vs Reaction Time

Before building the physical robot, MATLAB/Simulink was used to simulate the PID controller's response to initial tilt disturbances. This helped validate the control strategy and estimate optimal PID parameters.

Simulation Setup

  • Initial tilt angle: 25 degrees from vertical
  • PID gains: Kp = 60, Ki = 0, Kd = 6.0
  • Sampling rate: 200 Hz (5ms loop time)
  • Motor response modeled with first-order lag

Key Observations

  • Recovery time: ~1 second to reach vertical equilibrium
  • Overshoot: Minimal overshoot (<2°) due to derivative damping
  • Settling time: Stable within 1.5 seconds
  • No steady-state error with Ki = 0 for small disturbances

MATLAB simulation showing robot recovering from 25° initial tilt

Why Simulation Matters: Physical testing is expensive and risky — motors can overheat, components can break, and untuned PID can cause violent oscillations. MATLAB simulation allows rapid parameter iteration without hardware risk, reducing development time from weeks to hours.

Software Implementations

Two Simulation Environments

The self-balancing robot was implemented and tested in two distinct simulation environments, each serving different purposes in the development and validation process.

1. OpenGL Python Simulator

A custom-built 3D visualization using Python and OpenGL library provides real-time interactive control and physics-based simulation of the balancing robot.

Key Features

  • Full Manual Control: Arrow keys for forward/backward/turn movements
  • Real-time PID Tuning: Adjust Kp, Ki, Kd parameters during runtime
  • Physics Engine: Custom implementation of inverted pendulum dynamics
  • Visual Feedback: 3D robot model with tilt angle visualization
  • Sensor Simulation: Simulated MPU6050 gyroscope and accelerometer
  • Lightweight: Runs on any system with Python and OpenGL support

Purpose

  • Rapid PID parameter testing and optimization
  • Algorithm validation before hardware deployment
  • Educational demonstration of control theory
  • Safe testing of extreme scenarios without hardware risk

🔍 Click to enlarge - OpenGL 3D Visualization

🔍 Click to enlarge - Webots Restaurant Scene

2. Webots Professional Simulator

Webots provides a realistic restaurant environment where the self-balancing robot operates as an autonomous service robot, navigating between tables while maintaining balance.

Key Features

  • Realistic Environment: Complete restaurant with tables, chairs, obstacles
  • Physics Accuracy: Professional-grade physics engine (ODE)
  • Autonomous Navigation: Path planning and obstacle avoidance
  • Multi-Sensor Fusion: Distance sensors, cameras, IMU integration
  • Real-world Constraints: Floor friction, wind disturbances simulation
  • Supervisor Control: Monitor performance metrics in real-time

Purpose

  • Test robot in practical application scenario
  • Validate balance control under navigation constraints
  • Simulate real-world service robot deployment
  • Demonstrate integration of multiple subsystems

Implementation Comparison

OpenGL Python Simulator

  • ✓ Fast iteration and testing
  • ✓ Easy PID parameter tuning
  • ✓ Manual control for learning
  • ✓ Lightweight and portable
  • ✓ Custom physics implementation

Webots Professional

  • ✓ High-fidelity physics simulation
  • ✓ Real-world environment testing
  • ✓ Industry-standard robotics platform
  • ✓ Autonomous navigation testing
  • ✓ Multi-robot coordination capability
Outcomes

Results & Observations

RESULT 01
Balance Achieved
Robot successfully maintains balance for small disturbances — the primary objective achieved with the PID loop.
RESULT 02
PID Gain Sensitivity
PID gains critically affect stability. Incorrect tuning leads to oscillation or divergence — empirical tuning essential.
RESULT 03
Derivative Damping
The D-term significantly reduced oscillation amplitude by providing velocity-proportional damping via gyroscope feedback.
RESULT 04
Sensor Noise Impact
MPU6050 noise affected raw performance. The complementary filter substantially improved overall balance stability.
